title: Bank Specialization and Zombie Lending
abstract: We study whether banks internalize congestion externalities when lending to zombie firms. We conjecture that banks should be better informed about the presence of zombie firms and the congestion externalities that such firms exert on healthy borrowers in industries where banks are specialized and show that banks’ credit supply to zombie firms relates negatively to their industry specialization. This relation is stronger when congestion externalities are likely to have stronger adverse effects, namely when zombie firms take a higher fraction of resources in the industry or when the industry is geographically more concentrated. Additionally, this relation is weaker in industries with higher asset specificity as zombie firms’ default (and potential asset fire sales) could reduce healthy borrowers’ collateral value.

author.Olivier De Jonghe: Research Department, National Bank of Belgium, 1000 Brussels, Belgium; and Finance Department, Tilburg University, 5000LE Tilburg, Netherlands
author.Klaas Mulier: Department of Accounting, Corporate Finance and Taxation, Ghent University, 9000 Gent, Belgium
author.Ilia Samarin: Research Department, National Bank of Belgium, 1000 Brussels, Belgium

article.1. Introduction: Zombie firms are poorly performing firms that are unable to service their debt from operating cash flows over a prolonged period of time but survive thanks to lax credit supply by lenders. Such zombie lending can have a large negative economic impact. For instance, it is known to have contributed to the “lost decade” of Japan’s economy (Caballero et al. 2008). Unfortunately, zombie firms have become more prevalent in many European countries over the last decades (Banerjee and Hofmann 2022), and this rise in zombie firms has been put forward as an explanation for Europe’s low productivity (Gouveia and Osterhold 2018,McGowan et al. 2018), low innovation and patenting activity (Schmidt et al. 2023), and lack of inflation (Acharya et al. 2024) during the recovery from the financial and sovereign debt crisis. In terms of drivers of zombie lending, the literature has mainly proposed strategic motivations rooted in either (i) avoiding bank capital shortfalls (Blattner et al. 2023), (ii) gambling for resurrection (Bruche and Llobet 2014), or (iii) taking advantage of asymmetric information to offload bad borrowers to market-based lenders (Hu and Varas 2021). The literature on zombie lending mitigation has thus far documented on-site inspections of bank regulators (Passalacqua et al. 2020,Bonfim et al. 2023) and more efficient corporate insolvency rules (Becker and Ivashina 2022). In this paper, we look into a novel, information-based mechanism that might induce banks toreducecredit supply to zombie firms, namely bank specialization. The mechanism has two ingredients. First, zombie firms can create negative congestion externalities on healthy borrowers. Such externalities arise when zombie firms are not forced to downsize and therefore take too much market share in the input and output market (Caballero et al. 2008) and hence put pressure on healthy borrowers by keeping wages high and markups low (Acharya et al. 2024). If so, keeping zombie firms alive will deteriorate the bank’s lending portfolio, not only directly through having zombie firms in the portfolio, but also indirectly because the performance of the bank’s healthy borrowers will deteriorate if they have to compete with zombie firms. Second, to understand whether congestion externalities occur or not, banks need to have industry-specific knowledge. This type of knowledge cannot be inferred through firm-specific hard or soft information but can be learned through having many interactions with borrowers from the same industry, that is, through specializing (Berger et al. 2017,De Jonghe et al. 2020). Banks thus have an incentive to internalize congestion externalities created by zombie firms but are only likely to do so if they are aware of these externalities on healthy borrowers and are able to identify the zombie firms. To investigate this mechanism, we use a unique data set that combines three data sources of the National Bank of Belgium (Belgium’s Central Bank). We draw firm-bank level credit information for the universe of banks and firms in Belgium from the Corporate Credit Register and augment these data with firms’ and banks’ balance sheets and income statements. As banks are most informed about industries where they grant most loans, banks’ industry specialization is often proxied by an industry’s share in the bank’s lending portfolio (De Jonghe et al. 2020,Blickle et al. 2021,Degryse et al. 2021). The richness of the data enables us to use a comprehensive set of fixed effects. Building onKhwaja and Mian (2008), we disentangle credit demand from credit supply using firm × year fixed effects. Thus, in all our specifications, our identification is based on comparing how multiple banks with different levels of industry specialization adjust their credit supply to thesamefirm/zombie. Our final data set runs from 2004 to 2018 and covers 614,322 firm-bank-year observations for 54 banks and more than 50,000 firms. Our results at the extensive margin show that, holding credit demand constant, zombie firms are less likely to receive a new loan from a bank that is specialized in lending to the zombie’s industry than from a bank that is not specialized. Results at the intensive margin confirm this. For a given level of demand, a zombie firm sees its credit supply significantly more reduced from the bank that is relatively more specialized in lending to the zombie’s industry. Our specifications also include bank × year fixed effects to absorb bank-time–specific shocks and time-invariant bank characteristics. Our results can thus also be interpreted as follows: Banks reduce their credit supply significantly more to zombie firms in industries in which they are specialized than to zombie firms in industries in which they are not specialized. Both interpretations, however, are in line with the idea that banks are more aware and concerned about congestion externalities of zombie lending in industries where they are specialized. Next, we shed further light on the characteristics of the bank specialization and zombie lending mechanism. Healthy borrowers compete with zombie firms for resources and market share. This competition is likely only distorted if zombie firms make up a meaningful fraction of the industry and hence only leads to congestion externalities in those cases. We show that the effect of bank specialization on zombie lending is significantly stronger in industries where the share of labor employed at zombie firms is higher.1Second, as some resources are rather immobile (e.g., labor), congestion externalities are likely to be larger when healthy borrowers and zombie firms compete very locally. In line with this, we find that the effect of bank specialization on zombie lending is significantly stronger in geographically concentrated industries. Third, congestion externalities are potentially more costly for healthy borrowers when they are more likely to have good investment opportunities (as they might need to forego some). We show that the effect of bank specialization on zombie lending is significantly stronger in industries that are expected to exhibit higher sales growth. Fourth, a zombie firm’s default may pose contagion risk for the collateral value of healthy borrowers’ assets if the zombie’s assets are sold at fire sale prices. This is more likely to occur in industries with higher asset specificity as these assets are less redeployable outside the industry and therefore have lower liquidation and collateral values (Acharya et al. 2007,Almeida et al. 2011,Giannetti and Saidi 2019). Thanks to their information advantage, specialized banks should be more aware of this and take it into account when lending to zombie firms. We show that the effect of bank specialization on zombie lending is weaker in industries with higher asset specificity. These results cannot be fully rationalized by a mechanism where specialized banks only have an information advantage in detecting zombie firms and want to reduce their exposure to such firms, because we find that specialized banks react stronger to zombie firms when these have a larger impact on healthy borrowers. Therefore, the mechanism also needs to imply superior knowledge about congestion externalities and willingness to internalize these. Indeed, if it were only about detecting zombie firms, then the degree to which zombie firms exert externalities on healthy borrowers should not matter for a specialized bank’s willingness to reduce zombie lending. A concern one could have is that our specialization measure could be capturing a bank’s industry market share. In that case, a bank’s power to avoid cascade effects and industry-wide distress could be driving our results, and we would then be documenting the same mechanism asGiannetti and Saidi (2019). This is not the case. Empirically, the correlation between banks’ industry specialization and market share is weak (only −0.08),2and controlling for banks’ industry market share interacted with firms’ zombie status does not matter for our results. Conceptually,Giannetti and Saidi (2019)use syndicated loan data to analyze bank behavior when an entire industry faces distress. There is little information asymmetry as syndicated loans concern very large firms and industry distress is publicly observed. It is hence unlikely that industry-specific soft information (gained from specializing) would be relevant for banks’ lending decision in their context. In contrast, it is not said that the market-share channel could not be relevant in our context. In fact, our analyses show that high-market-share banks are more likely to internalize zombie congestion externalities than low-market-share banks, but also that our results on banks’ industry specialization and zombie lending remain entirely unaffected. This confirms that gaining industry-specific knowledge through specialization is important and that this information mechanism is orthogonal to market share. Relatedly, as industry specialization implies higher exposure to an industry, it might also create concentration risk. However, we deem it unlikely that our specialization results can be explained by this alone. With concentration risk, banks care about downside risk, which does not resonate with our results being stronger in industries with higher expected sales growth. Furthermore, we design a test that separates specialization from exposure risk (and controls for it) and find that our results are consistent with the information-based interpretation but not the high exposure interpretation. Another possible concern is to what extent the information captured in industry specialization is distinct from borrower-specific hard or soft information. To look at this, we augment our baseline model with bank-firm relationship characteristics (e.g., firm exposure risk, relationship length, main bank status) and their interaction with firms’ zombie status, as well as with interactions between banks’ industry specialization and firms’ financial characteristics (e.g., profitability, leverage). These controls have little to no impact on the baseline results, confirming that our results are driven by a wider industry-specific knowledge as opposed to specific firm-related information. After these robustness checks, we move beyond the exclusive focus on bank lending as an outcome variable and examine the implications of banks’ industry specialization and zombie congestion for the (risk-adjusted) performance of banks’ loan portfolios. We document that banks have an incentive to eliminate zombie congestion and to specialize in lending to an industry. We find that zombie congestion in an industry is associated with lower risk-adjusted returns on banks’ loan portfolio to healthy firms, whereas specialization is associated with higher risk-adjusted returns. Furthermore, our paper proposes some important modifications to the widely used Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) definition to identify zombie firms using firms’ financial statements. The OECD measure (McGowan et al. 2018) defines zombie firms as mature firms whose EBIT-to-interest expense ratio is below one for three consecutive years. Our first modification is to start from firms’ recurring cash flows rather than from firms’ earnings before interest and tax (EBIT). This implies adding back depreciation and amortization as well as including recurring financial revenues (e.g., from T-bills or intercompany loans). This modification avoids wrongly classifying as zombies: (i) firms with recent large capital expenditures and hence large depreciation, (ii) firms with high levels of cash equivalents/short-term investments, and (iii) firms that grant many intercompany loans. Our second modification is to not look at three consecutive years, but to look at whether the three-year cumulative recurring cash flows fail to cover the three-year cumulative interest expenses (where also minimum two of the three individual years fail). This brings more persistence to zombie firms identified at the margin as otherwise one (exceptional) year where the ratio > 1, implies that a firm will be considered healthy for the next three years. Compared with the OECD definition, our definition estimates a much smaller and less cyclical share of zombie borrowers in the economy: 3.4% instead of 10.4%. This difference is the result of 8% of borrowers that are zombies according to the OECD measure but not ours, and 1% that are zombies according to our measure but not the OECD’s. The average zombie identified according to our definition is of significantly lower quality and less viable than the average zombie identified according to the OECD definition. The differences in quality pertain to firm profitability, leverage, having negative equity, past firm growth, future sales growth, investments in tangible assets, and so on. These modifications not only show a more credible picture of zombie firms, but we show that they are also important to identify the role that bank specialization plays in zombie lending. UnlikeCaballero et al. (2008)andAcharya et al. (2019,2024), our zombie measure does not require zombie firms to be charged interest rates below benchmark risk-free rates (i.e., of AA-rated firms). The zombie firms in our sample pay an (implicit) interest rate that is on average 125 basis points higher than the healthy firms.3However, we show that this is still in line with receiving an interest rate subsidy. A back-of-the-envelope calculation likeDe Marco et al. (2021)indicates that banks grant zombie firms an interest rate that is 145 basis points lower than what would be expected based on their risk profile (i.e., their interest rate should on average actually be 270 basis points higher instead of only 125). Nonetheless, we perform two robustness tests related to this. First, we split our zombie firms into two groups based on the zombie firms’ median implicit interest rate and rerun our baseline model. The results hold equally for both groups of zombie firms, irrespective of whether their interest rates are the most subsidized or not. Second, we identify zombie firms as inAcharya et al. (2019)and rerun our baseline model. Also here, the effect of bank specialization on zombie lending is confirmed. We contribute to the academic literature in at least three ways. First, we contribute to the literature on zombie lending. Most studies focus on the role of bank capital (Caballero et al. 2008;Giannetti and Simonov 2013;Acharya et al. 2019,2024) or overall bank health (Andrews and Petroulakis 2017,Storz et al. 2017). Recently,Bonfim et al. (2023)andPassalacqua et al. (2020)show that on-site regulatory inspections also significantly determine banks’ incentives to renew loans to zombie firms. We show a novel determinant that alleviates zombie lending, namely bank specialization. Secondly, we contribute to the literature on bank industry specialization. One view is that banks should diversify their lending portfolio (Diamond 1984). However, recent theoretical arguments and empirical evidence claim substantial benefits of specialization. Industry specialization gives banks an information advantage which may reduce screening and monitoring costs (Berger et al. 2017) and allows banks to offer loans with more generous terms (Blickle et al. 2021) and less restrictive covenants (Giometti et al. 2023). Banks have incentives to protect borrowers in specialized industries to maintain their information advantage (Müller 2020), and indeed, did so during the interbank freeze after the Lehman Brothers collapse (De Jonghe et al. 2020). Bank specialization has also been shown to correlate with lower credit risk (Jahn et al. 2016), higher performance (Acharya et al. 2006), higher distance-to-default (Beck et al. 2022), higher financial stability (Acharya et al. 2006,Beck et al. 2022), and expertise (Degryse et al. 2021). Our results are in line with this recent evidence and show that bank specialization relates to lower zombie lending to reduce the risk of negative congestion externalities onto healthy borrowers. Finally, we add to the debate on the definition of zombie firms (Caballero et al. 2008,McGowan et al. 2018,Acharya et al. 2019,Schivardi et al. 2020,Bonfim et al. 2023). Although the firms identified as zombies according to our definition pay a higher interest rate than healthy firms, there does seem to be some subsidization (Caballero et al. 2008,Acharya et al. 2019) as the difference in interest rates is less than what the difference in firm risk would imply (De Marco et al. 2021,Müller et al. 2021). Moreover, although we do not impose a solvency criterion in our zombie definition, we are picking up firms with higher leverage (Acharya et al. 2024) that are more likely to have negative equity (Bonfim et al. 2023) and have lower growth potential (Banerjee and Hofmann 2022) due to lower investment in physical and human capital and lower future sales growth. Also importantly, we end up with a more moderate size of the zombie population. This is in line with recent studies showing that zombie firms in certain countries are not as prevalent as previously believed (Rodano and Sette (2019)for evidence on Italy orFavara et al. (2021)for the United States).
article.2. Data: We draw data from three unique data sources available at the National Bank of Belgium (NBB). The first source is the Corporate Credit Register. It records, at a monthly frequency, all loans granted by Belgian financial institutions to firms operating in Belgium. This database allows us (i) to compute credit growth at the intensive and extensive margin, (ii) to isolate credit supply from credit demand by (mainly) focusing on multiple-bank borrowers, and (iii) to compute banks’ industry specialization in the corporate credit market. We aggregate the credit exposures at the firm-bank-year level before matching them with the second data source. The second data source is the Central Balance Sheet Office. It collects balance sheets and income statements ofthe universeof Belgian enterprises. All firms, irrespective of their size, have to file financial statements on an annual basis. This data set also contains a firm’s date of incorporation, legal form, and the main economic industry a firm operates in. The richness and completeness of this database allow us to construct our new zombie definition. Finally, we obtain balance sheets and income statements of financial institutions. Banks report these data following the so-called Scheme A, and we use the latest financial statement reported in a given year. We apply the following filters. Because we analyze bank lending to the real economy, we exclude firms from the financial and insurance industry, public administration and education industries, activities of extraterritorial entities, and activities of households as employers (NACE4industries K, O, P, T, U). Additionally, we exclude microfirms from the analysis due to different reporting requirements. Micro firms are defined as firms who have, on average over the sample period, less than 10 FTEs and less than 350,000 euro in total assets. We also exclude rare and highly specific legal forms.5Finally, as there was a reporting threshold of 25,000 Euro before April 2012, we exclude credit exposures below 25,000 Euro to be consistent throughout all sampled years (2004–2018). However, these excluded exposures account only for 1% of total corporate credit. Merging these three sources and applying the aforementioned filters results in a data set containing54banks lending to more than50,000multiple-bank firms over the sample period 2004 to 2018. All tables and figures in this paper are based on this sample of multiple-bank borrowers unless explicitly stated otherwise. article.3. Who Are the Zombie Firms?: A zombie firm is a mature, consistently underperforming, and unproductive firm that is not able to repay its debt. These firms have a marginal return on capital that is below the risk-adjusted market cost of capital (Schivardi et al. 2022) and would, barring external support, exit the market in a frictionless economy. Because of market failures, zombie firms can stay alive, and banks are often blamed for helping them. Although there is more or less agreement on the definition of zombie firms, there is an abundance of empirical proxies. Caballero et al. (2008)identify zombie firms as those who received subsidized credit, which is being defined as a lower interest rate than the interest rate paid by the highest quality (AA-rated) borrowers.Andrews and Petroulakis (2017)andMcGowan et al. (2018)define zombies as firms that are at least 10 years old and have an interest coverage ratio (earnings before interests and taxes (EBIT) to financial expenses) below one for three consecutive years. We refer to this as the OECD definition. Other researchers have identified zombie firms using (a combination of) firm performance measures, such as (i) firm profitability (Schivardi et al. 2022), (ii) negative equity (Bonfim et al. 2023), (iii) low interest coverage ratio (Andrews and Petroulakis 2017), or (iv) leverage (Acharya et al. 2024). 3.1. New Approach to Classify Zombie Firms We suggest a new approach to identify zombie firms. Our approach is based onrecurring cash flowsandlonger-term structural underperformanceand builds on the OECD definition. A firm is classified as a zombie if three conditions are met: (i) the firm’s three-year accumulated recurring cash flows fail to cover three-year accumulated interest expenses, (ii) minimum two of the three individual years (i.e., not accumulated) also fail, and (iii) the firm is at least 10 years old. We focus onrecurring cash flows, which implies two departures from the OECD definition. The first difference is the use of Earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization (EBITDA) rather than EBIT to measure recurring operational income. As interest expenses are a cash outflow, operational earnings (EBIT) is not a good indicator of a firm’s ability to service its debt due to its inclusion of components that do not generate cash-flows, in particular depreciation and amortization. Furthermore, depreciation and amortization practices are highly industry specific (see Figure C.1 in the Online Appendix where we plot the industry-average share of depreciation and amortization in EBITDA). This distorts comparisons across industries and countries (to the extent that countries differ in their industrial composition). Finally, firms with high depreciation in the current period (and thus low EBIT relative to EBITDA) tend to have invested heavily in the previous years, which is counterintuitive as zombie firms are expected not to be able to invest (Storz et al. 2017). The second difference is that we add recurring financial income to operating income to measure a firm’s recurring cash flows. Recurring financial income (of which dividends and income earned on current assets are the most important sources) can also be used to service interest expenses. In addition, recurring financial revenues are more prevalent in large firms, and excluding these revenues might induce biases and inflate the share of capital and labor stuck in zombies. Finally, recurring financial revenues are industry-specific and should thus be properly accounted for (Figure C.1 in the Online Appendix plots the industry-average share of recurring financial revenues in EBITDA). Furthermore, our identification requires that recurring cash flows arestructurallyinsufficient to meet interest expenses. Rather than assessing the condition year-by-year over a three-year horizon (as in the OECD concept), we assess whether three-year cumulative recurring cash flows are insufficient to cover three-year cumulative interest expenses. Using cumulative recurring cash flows and interest expenses over a period of three years is akin toSchivardi et al. (2022), who suggest using a moving average–based approach for the numerator and denominator of the interest coverage ratio. With this adjustment, we avoid incorrectly classifying firms as healthy, if they (possibly by luck) have a one-off improvement in cash flows or one-off reduction in interest expenses.6Moreover, in zombie definitions that require a threshold not to be breached for three consecutive years, one (exceptional) year that does breach it implies that a firm will not be considered as a zombie for the next three years. To avoid, on the other hand, incorrectly classifying firms as zombies because of one exceptionally bad year, we additionally impose that recurring cash flows should be less than interest expenses in at least two of the three years. Each adjustment may have important consequences for classifying a firm as a zombie or not. To shed light on the impact of these adjustments, we plot inFigure 1, the impact of each of these adjustments on the share of zombies borrowers. Starting from the OECD definition (solid line on top), we see that adding financial revenues (long-dash) already has a sizeable impact on the number of firms identified as zombies. Further, using cash flows (EBITDA) rather than EBIT substantially reduces the number and importance of zombies (see alsoRodano and Sette (2019)for a similar impact when applied to Italian firms). Finally, assessing cash flow deficiencies over a three-year horizon, rather than on a year-by-year basis, leads to a substantial increase in the number of zombie firms (but still substantially less than using the OECD definition). It is important to mention that these adjustments mainly lead to a level shift in the number of zombies, whereas the time series evolution of our measure (solid line with triangles) and the OECD measure (top solid line) is very similar. Prior to the global financial crisis, the share of zombie borrowers was decreasing, followed by a run-up leading to a peak in 2011 and a gradual decrease afterward. The amplitude of the cycle is, however, smaller when using our definition. 3.2. Zombie Firms vs. Low-Quality Firms In the previous section, we introduced our zombie definition and showed what the adjustments implied for the number of firms identified as zombies (vis-à-vis the OECD definition). In this section, we document what these adjustments imply for the type of firms identified as zombies. Comparing zombies according to our definition (Z) with the OECD definition (ZOECDZOECD) partitions the sample into four groups: (i) firms identified as zombies under both definitions, (ii) firms identified as zombies according to our definition but as healthy according to the OECD definition, (iii) firms identified as zombies using the OECD definition, but as healthy according to our definition, and (iv) healthy firms under both approaches. The number of firms in each group is, respectively, 2.4%, 1.0%, 8.0%, and 88.6% of the sample (275,122 firm-year observations). Using regression analysis, we are comparing whether and how firms in each of these four groups differ in terms of solvency and risk, profitability, growth of tangible assets and employment, future sales growth, and cost of debt (i.e., implicit interest rates). The regression analysis includes Industry × Year and Region × Year fixed effects to net out industry or regional differences in firm characteristics and performance. The results are reported inTable 2, and the reported coefficients should be interpreted as deviations from the baseline group, that is, firms identified as zombies under both definitions (Z = 1 andZOECDZOECD= 1). First, our approach picks up a set of firms (Z = 1 andZOECDZOECD= 0), representing 1% of the sampled firms, which are very similar (both economically and statistically) to the baseline group, that is, zombies under both definitions. However, under the OECD definition, these firms would be pooled together with the set of healthy firms (last row), from which they differ substantially and significantly. Second, and importantly, a sizeable number of firms (8.0% of the sample) are classified as zombies according to the OECD definition but not according to ours (Z = 0 andZOECDZOECD= 1). We find that these firms substantially outperform the benchmark group in terms of solvency, profitability, investment, employment growth, and future sales growth and have a lower cost of debt (i.e., pay 98 basis points lower implicit interest rates). In fact, their characteristics are much closer to those of healthy firms than those of zombie firms. They could thus be considered as lower-quality healthy firms much rather than zombie firms. Third, compared with the baseline group, firms that are classified as healthy under both definitions (i.e., Z = 0 andZOECDZOECD= 0) are substantially better capitalized, have a much lower incidence of firms with negative equity, and are substantially more profitable, resulting in much higher Altman Z scores. These healthy firms also invest more in physical and human capital, exhibit better future performance (as measured by sales growth), and have a lower cost of debt. The results inTable 2also allow us to make some qualitative comparisons with studies using alternative concepts to identify zombies. Without imposing a solvency criterion in our zombie definition, we are picking up firms with higher leverage (Acharya et al. 2024) that are more likely to have negative equity (Bonfim et al. 2023) and have lower growth potential (Banerjee and Hofmann 2022) due to lower investment in physical and human capital and lower future sales growth. Furthermore, we show that the zombie definition used by the OECD mixes zombie firms with low-quality, but still healthy, firms. Interestingly, the ratio of zombies (Z = 1) to zombies and low-quality firms (Z = 1 andZOECDZOECD= 1) is about 30%. Using a different zombie measurement approach,Acharya et al. (2024)obtain a similar conclusion using a European sample of firms. Caballero et al. (2008)andAcharya et al. (2019,2024) require zombie firms to be charged interest rates below that of AA-rated firms. Although our zombie definition does not impose such a requirement, we do believe that the firms we identify as zombies are receiving a meaningful interest rate subsidy despite paying a higher interest rate than healthy firms. A back-of-the-envelope calculation7similar toDe Marco et al. (2021)indicates that banks should charge zombie firms 270 basis points more than healthy firms on their debt borrowing to have the same risk-adjusted return on both type of firms.8The actual difference in the data are 125 basis points, suggesting a 145 basis points subsidy. The firms we identify as zombies thus pay an interest rate that is (significantly) lower than justified by their risk, which is consistent with a “weak” concept of subsidization. 3.3. Resources Sunk in Zombies and Impact on Healthy Firms Figure 1already showed the dynamics of the fraction of zombie borrowers in Belgium between 2004 and 2018. InFigure 2, we plot the evolution of resources allocated to (or sunk in) zombies and depict three lines (in addition to the fraction of zombie borrowers): the share of capital (fixed tangible assets), labor force (Full Time Equivalents (FTEs)), and bank credit sunk in zombie borrowers. We can see an increase in the zombie population during and after the financial crisis in the years 2008–2011. After that, the zombie population started to decline slowly. The rise of zombies during the crisis has also been found in other European countries (McGowan et al. 2018). The share of credit sunk in zombies exhibit similar dynamics as the share of zombie borrowers but with a larger amplitude. In most years, the share of capital and labor sunk in zombies is less than the share of credit allocated to zombies. On the one hand, it is reassuring that less real resources than financial resources are sunk in zombies. On the other hand, the credit sunk in zombies (although less used for investments in physical and human capital) may negatively affect the performance of healthy firms competing with these zombies (for credit and market share); a phenomenon calledzombie congestion. Zombie congestion may happen through at least two channels. First, a larger amount of credit granted to zombie borrowers limits the amount of credit available to healthy firms that are willing to take a bank loan. Second, as bank credit helps zombie firms to stay alive and take up the market share, it slows down business dynamism and negatively affects competition (McGowan et al. 2018) and mark-ups (Acharya et al. 2024). Prior research shows a robust, negative relationship between the growth of healthy firms and the share of zombie firms in their industry (seeCaballero et al. (2008)who show this for Japan andMcGowan et al. (2018)who document it for European Union (EU) firms). Importantly, there is substantial variation in the share of zombie borrowers and zombie credit across years and industries as can be seen in the histogram plot in Figure E.1 in the Online Appendix. In Table F.1 in the Online Appendix, we document that zombie congestion effects are present in the Belgian data as well. Healthy borrowers exhibit higher growth in tangible assets, in employment, in current sales and in future sales than zombies. However, the growth gap will be smaller if healthy borrowers operate in industries where a larger fraction of credit is granted to zombie firms.Schivardi et al. (2020,2022) document a serious, and difficult to solve, identification problem in such regressions. They even conclude that the correlation between healthy firms’ performance and zombie presence might be mechanical and biased. Solving this identification problem is not the purpose of the paper, and therefore, the results in Table F.1 in the Online Appendix are indicative and only present correlations and not causal evidence. Moreover, as the direction and magnitude of the bias are unclear and context specific (Schivardi et al. 2020,2022), assessing the economic magnitude of the coefficients is meaningless.
article.4. Bank Specialization and Credit Supply: 4.1. Empirical Specification Our goal is to analyze the role of bank specialization in zombie lending. To be more specific, we are investigating whether the effect of bank specialization on credit supply is different for zombie firms than for healthy firms. Econometrically, we estimate the following baseline equation: The dependent variableyibtis one of three measures of credit growth. First,New Loanis a dummy variable that equals one if credit growth for a firmiwith bankbin yeartis positive and zero otherwise. It measures, for existing borrowers, whether a new loan has been added or an existing limit has been increased. Next, we measure intensive margin credit growth in two alternative ways.ΔLn(Credit)is the annual credit growth, defined as the logarithmic difference between the outstanding credit amount, at the firm-bank level, in yeartand yeart− 1.ΔCredit/Assetsis the absolute change in the outstanding credit amount, at the firm-bank level, from yeart− 1 to yeartscaled by the borrower’s total assets int− 1. With these measures, we only focus on theintensivemargin of lending, that is, changes in the volume of lending to existing borrowers, as the concern is that banks keep zombie firms alive (rather than starting new relationships with zombie firms). Zitis a zombie identifier, which equals one if a firmiis identified as a zombie according to our definition in yeart. Bank specialization,𝑆𝑝𝑒𝑐𝑖𝑎𝑙𝑖𝑧𝑎𝑡𝑖𝑜𝑛𝑏𝑗𝑡−1Specializationbjt−1, or industry lending concentration, is the fraction of bankb’s credit granted to an industryjin bankb’s total corporate lending portfolio, measured in yeart− 1. An industryjis measured at the two-digit level of the NACE classification. We interact the zombie identifier with bank specialization to grasp whether the effect of specialization on credit supply differs for zombie firms.Table 1and Figure G.1 in the Online Appendix show that there is sizeable variation in industry specialization across the sample but also between banks within an industry-year. Given the inclusion of a large set of fixed effects, see infra, it is the latter source of variation that is relevant for estimating and identifying the effect of specialization on credit supply to zombie firms. The baseline specification inEquation (1)includes an extensive set of fixed effects. We resort to the most stringent specification using the methodology ofKhwaja and Mian (2008). Firm × Year fixed effects (νit) capture any observed and unobserved firm-specific credit demand effects. Doing so, we only include firms that simultaneously borrow from two or more banks. This is stringent in the setting of Belgium, as many firms in Belgium borrow from only one bank. However, as credit demand might differ substantially between zombie firms and healthy firms, it is crucial to isolate demand from supply and therefore resort to the approach ofKhwaja and Mian (2008). This Firm × Year fixed effect also absorbs the direct effect ofZitonyibtand is therefore not separately included inEquation (1). Additionally, we include Bank × Year fixed effects (𝜐𝑏⁢𝑡υb⁢t) to capture all observed and unobserved bank-specific determinants of lending. The coefficients of interest areβ1andβ2.β1is expected to be positive. Banks tend to provide more credit to firms operating in industries in which the bank is specialized (De Jonghe et al. 2020). The coefficientβ2of the interaction between specialization and the zombie identifier is expected to be negative. A negative sign indicates that zombie firms benefit less (or are penalized) in terms of credit supply because of bank specialization. Alternatively, a zombie firm borrowing from two banks will experience a lower credit supply from its specialized lender vis-à-vis its uninformed lender. 4.2. Main Results Table 3presents the results of estimatingEquation (1)on the sample of multiple-bank borrowers over the period 2004–2018. The sample includes614,322bank-firm-year observations. Bank specialization has been standardized in the regressions (to have a mean of zero and standard deviation of one) to facilitate the interpretation and economic impact assessment. We find, first, a positive and statistically significant coefficient of banks’ industry specialization. This is in line with existing evidence and suggests that banks are on average more likely to increase lending to firms in industries they specialize in. Second, and of crucial interest for our analysis, is the negative coefficient on the interaction term between industry specialization and the zombie indicator, in each of the three columns. In the first column, the coefficient on the interaction term is smaller (in absolute value) than the main effect of specialization, indicating that the beneficial effect of specialization on credit supply is much less pronounced for zombie borrowers. Zombie borrowers are less likely to receive a new loan from their specialized lender compared with their less specialized lender. The effects of specialization are also economically significant. A one-standard-deviation increase in specialization is, for a healthy firm, associated with a 2.01 percentage point higher probability of obtaining a new loan (from its current lenders). This is sizeable, given that the average probability of receiving a new loan in the sample is 25.61% (see the summary statistics inTable 1). However, the results in column 1 ofTable 3also suggest that for zombie firms, the impact of a one standard deviation increase in specialization on the probability of receiving a new loan is 1.53 percentage points lower for zombie firms compared with healthy firms. This is economically meaningful as it corresponds to a 6% lower probability compared with the unconditional average of 25.61% and thus shows that zombie borrowers face stricter credit supply (relative to healthy firms) at the extensive margin from specialized banks. In the second and third column, the coefficients on the interaction term are again negative and statistically significant. This implies that an increase in industry specialization also reduces credit for zombie borrowers on the intensive margin. The results in column 2 show that a one standard deviation increase in a bank’s industry specialization widens the gap in credit growth between healthy and zombie firms by 0.85 percentage points. This is economically meaningful as this widening corresponds with approximately 44% of the unconditional average annual credit growth (−1.91). In the case of the credit growth relative to firm size (column 3), whereas a one-standard-deviation increase in bank specialization is associated with an average increase in relative credit amount of almost 0.11 percentage points (which is well above the sample mean of credit growth), a zombie borrower from the same industry faces a credit reduction, which is twice larger in magnitude (−0.23 = 0.11 − 0.34). Overall, our findings reveal an important role of bank industry specialization in zombie lending. On the one hand, more credit is granted to borrowers operating in industries in which banks are more specialized. This finding is in line with the existing literature (De Jonghe et al. 2020,Paravisini et al. 2023), which shows that banks expand lending to industries where they are more specialized. On the other hand, we shed light on a novel aspect of bank industry specialization that has not been discussed before. We find that bank specialization is associated with lower zombie lending. Using our sample of firms borrowing from multiple banks, we show that a zombie firm faces a lower credit supply at the bank that is more specialized in the borrower’s industry. These main results are in line with the interpretation that banks gain an informational advantage from specialization. By concentrating lending in a certain industry, banks obtain more proprietary industry-specific information (Berger et al. 2017), which leads to a reduction in the cost of monitoring (Diamond 1984) and better screening abilities (Dell’Ariccia et al. 1999). As a result, banks specialized in a certain industry are (i) better able to identify zombie borrowers and distinguish them from healthy firms and firms who temporarily perform poorly, (ii) better able to assess the long-term financial prospects of their borrowers, and (iii) understand better the impact that zombie firms have on the long-term financial prospects of their healthy borrowers. InSection 8, we document in detail that this baseline specification isrobustto (i) firm-bank matching and credit demand, (ii) horse-racing low-quality firms with zombie firms, (iii) alternative measures of zombie firms (based on interest rate subsidization), (iv) alternative measures of specialization, (v) aggregate (size-weighted) analysis, (vi) confounding factors related to bank health and financial crises, and (vii) alternative clustering of standard errors.
article.5. Characteristics of the Specialization: If bank specialization provides banks with superior information about zombie congestion, banks’ incentives to reduce credit to zombie firms should be a function of how severe zombie firms’ externalities are for healthy borrowers. In this section, we explore various characteristics that help to understand how the bank specialization channel works. Scope for Zombie Congestion Externalities First, congestion externalities will likely be larger in industries where zombie firms are more dominant and take up a larger share of the output market or lock up more resources (such as labor, capital, and credit). Indeed, it is rather unlikely that healthy firms endure negative effects from competing with zombie firms if these zombie firms take up only a marginal fraction of resources and market share. Specialized banks should be more aware of this and therefore be especially likely to curb zombie lending in industries where zombies take up a meaningful share of resources and output. Opportunity Cost of Zombie Congestion Externalities Not only the scope for congestion externalities may matter but also the opportunity costs of those externalities. The cost of these externalities increases with the industry’s growth opportunities as the growth distortion due to zombie congestion is more palpable in those industries. Indeed, healthy firms are more likely to have good investment opportunities in faster growing industries and zombie congestion might force them to forego some of these opportunities. Hence credit reallocation from zombie to healthy firms is going to be especially valuable in industries with better growth prospects. Moreover, by allocating more credit to productive firms, banks can contribute to the industry productivity boost (Bai et al. 2018) and help to sustain the industry growth and benefit from it as it widens the pool of high-quality borrowers that would bring more positive net present value projects for a bank. Like in the previous analyses on the scope for congestion externalities, we thus find support for our hypothesis and significantly so on the intensive margin of credit supply. 5.3. Collateral Value Motive We now turn to a third channel, which induces banks tonotreduce lending to zombie firms (or to cut zombie lendingless) because triggering default of zombies is likely to hurt collateral values of healthy firms. Firms pledge assets as collateral to reduce the loss given default. However, when an asset is highly firm or industry specific it is less redeployable outside the industry and the bank’s repossession value of the collateral may be low when the firm defaults. If so, a firm’s default may even impact other firms with similar assets. When assets are more difficult to sell, then selling them at a substantial discount (fire-sale prices) drives down the market value of the collateral of other firms in the same industry (Acharya et al. 2007,Almeida et al. 2011,Giannetti and Saidi 2019).11A specialized bank might therefore be less willing to cut credit to zombie firms if it expects spillover effects to the collateral value of healthy borrowers if a zombie firm’s assets would be liquidated at a significant discount. Put differently, thanks to the information advantage due to specialization, a specialized bank is better aware of the consequences of collateral liquidation12on its other borrowers, which may affect the bank’s decision to lend to zombie borrowers. Alternative Explanations for the Characteristics of the Specialization Channel As is the case for many empirical papers, we cannot fully rule out that our results inSections 5.1–5.3are biased because the characteristics correlate with an omitted or unobserved industry characteristic that affects the relationship we test. We can only try to mitigate such concerns. In Online Appendix H, we explain and document all analyses we do to mitigate omitted variable concerns. For instance, one could wonder to what extent the documented characteristics are not simply picking up the same variation. To this end, first note that the correlation between the characteristics is rather modest. article.6. Confronting the Specialization Channe: We claim that industry specialization provides banks with unique industry-specific soft information that allows them to better screen and monitor borrowers and improves their understanding of industry dynamics. This rationale underpins our baseline hypothesis that specialized banks should be better at identifying zombie firms and understanding their impact on healthy borrowers, and therefore be more likely to internalize the externalities of lending to zombie firms. However, one could be concerned that our measure of banks’ industry specialization is picking up other sources of variation than the soft information that we refer to. If so, our results could potentially be explained by alternative hypotheses. In this section, we confront our claim with three competing hypotheses. 6.1. Internalization Incentives from Either Market Share or Concentration Risk Giannetti and Saidi (2019)show that banks are more likely to internalize lending externalities of industry distress if banks have a higher market share in the industry because high-market-share banks have (i) the scope to help the entire industry and (ii) rent extraction possibilities to protect. If our measure of specialization picks up variation related to banks’ market share in an industry, then this alternative explanation might bias our results. Another alternative explanation might be related to industry concentration risk. Although industry specialization is generally considered to reduce information asymmetries, one might object that specialized banks simply have a larger incentive to reduce lending to zombie firms because a larger share of their loans is going to this industry. Indeed, remember that we measureSpecializationas the total lending volume of bankbto industryjrelative to bankb’s total lending volume, in a given year. We try to disentangle the variation in our specialization measure due to an information advantage from that due to market share or an (over)exposure in the following way. First, we transformSpecializationinto aSpecialization(𝑡𝑜𝑝𝑑𝑒𝑐𝑖𝑙𝑒)(topdecile)dummy, which takes the value of one if the bank’s industry specialization is in the top decile of banks in that industry, and zero otherwise. This way, we get for each industry an indication of which banks have the highest specialization (and hence, relative information advantage) in the industry, while neglecting the actual level of the specialization measure (and thus neglecting the exposure that a certain level of specialization implies for the bank). Indeed, this dummy transformation treats specialization in smaller industries the same as specialization in larger industries and thus neglects the larger exposure that the latter typically imply for banks. This top decile cutoff is akin to using the cutoff as inParavisini et al. (2023)orBerthou et al. (2021). Second, we create a dummy variableExposure(𝑡𝑜𝑝𝑑𝑒𝑐𝑖𝑙𝑒)(topdecile)which takes the value one if the bank’s industry specialization is in the top decile of the bank, and zero otherwise. TheExposure(𝑡𝑜𝑝𝑑𝑒𝑐𝑖𝑙𝑒)(topdecile)dummy thus measures for each bank what its 10% largest industry exposures are. Third, although the correlation between banks’ industry specialization and industry market share is quite low in our sample (only −0.08) and therefore unlikely to have biased our main findings, we do want to formally test this. To do this, we create a dummy variable that captures the degree to which an industry depends on a particular bank, and hence, the degree to which a bank’s decision impacts the industry (Giannetti and Saidi 2019). ThisMarket Share(𝑡𝑜𝑝𝑑𝑒𝑐𝑖𝑙𝑒)(topdecile)dummy takes the value of one if the bank’s market share is in the top decile of the industry and zero otherwise. This variable thus identifies the ten percent most important banks for the industry. If all banks in our sample would be active in each industry, this measure would identify the five banks with the largest market share in each industry. To horserace our baseline hypothesis against these two competing explanations, we jointly includeSpecialization(𝑡𝑜𝑝𝑑𝑒𝑐𝑖𝑙𝑒)(topdecile),Exposure(𝑡𝑜𝑝𝑑𝑒𝑐𝑖𝑙𝑒)(topdecile), andMarket Share(𝑡𝑜𝑝𝑑𝑒𝑐𝑖𝑙𝑒)(topdecile), as well as an interaction between these and our zombie indicator, in the following regression model: As in the previous regression models, the direct effect of ourZombieindicator onyibtis subsumed in the Firm × Year fixed effects. If specialized banks reduce zombie lending because they are aware of zombie congestion externalities thanks to an information advantage, we expect this to be revealed by a significantβ2coefficient on the interaction termZ×Specialization(𝑡𝑜𝑝𝑑𝑒𝑐𝑖𝑙𝑒)(topdecile). If banks reduce zombie lending because they have a large exposure to industries, then we expect this to be revealed by a significantγ2coefficient on the interaction termZ×Exposure(𝑡𝑜𝑝𝑑𝑒𝑐𝑖𝑙𝑒)(topdecile). If banks reduce zombie lending because they have a large impact on an industry, then we expect this to be revealed by a significantδ2coefficient on the interaction termZ×Market Share(𝑡𝑜𝑝𝑑𝑒𝑐𝑖𝑙𝑒)(topdecile). As can be seen inTable 7, the interaction between a bank’s industry market share and the zombie indicator is always negative and statistically significant in four of the six specifications, suggesting that banks reduce lending to zombie firms more if they have a larger market share and hence larger impact on that industry. This is in line withGiannetti and Saidi (2019), who show that high-market-share banks are more likely to internalize externalities of industry downturns. In contrast, the interactionZ×Exposure(𝑡𝑜𝑝𝑑𝑒𝑐𝑖𝑙𝑒)(topdecile)is negative (five of six specifications) but never significantly different from zero. This means that we cannot confirm that banks reduce lending to zombie firms more in industries to which they have large exposures. Firm-Specific Soft and Hard Information Banks acquire soft information about their borrowers throughout the relationship (Petersen and Rajan 1994), and this has been shown to be an important determinant of firms’ access to credit (Petersen and Rajan 1995,Bolton et al. 2016). If banks lack expertise in an industry and are therefore deprived of industry-specific information, they may invest more in firm-specific information. The two sources of information could thus act as substitutes, and not adequately controlling for firm-specific soft information might bias our main finding. First, one might be concerned that our measure of industry specialization is picking up firm-specific information because it also includes lending to the firm itself. If a firm is large enough, it might simultaneously drive a bank’s industry specialization and its incentives to collect firm-specific information. Therefore, we introduce an alternative measure𝑆𝑝𝑒𝑐𝑖𝑎𝑙𝑖𝑧𝑎𝑡𝑖𝑜𝑛(𝑒𝑥𝑐𝑙𝑖)Specialization(excli), computed as the total lending volume of a bank to industryjexcluding the bank’s lending to firmi, relative to the bank’s total lending volume. Second, to further rule out that our main results are confounded by firm-specific information, we augment the baseline model with two measures of firm-bank-specific soft information that banks learn over the course of the relationships with their borrowers. On the one hand, by having closer and stronger relationships with their borrowers, banks are better able to screen the quality of the borrowers (Bolton et al. 2016). On the other hand, by having a long relationship with their borrowers, banks may even continue to lend to poorly performing firms if they believe that they can extract more profits from them later on (Sharpe 1990,Schäfer 2019). Hence, we introduce to the specification a variableLn(Relationship)that measures the relationship length since the first time a bank lent to a given borrower. Additionally, we control for private information that can be learned by the primary lender (Ioannidou and Ongena 2010) by adding a variableBank’s Share in Firm Creditthat measures the share of the bank in the firm’s total outstanding credit. What is important to note here is that these interaction terms are not to be interpreted as whether banks adjust credit supply differently to firms according to these firm characteristics (because we already include Firm × Year fixed effects which absorb these). It should, however, allow us to assess whether specialization induces banks to reduce zombie lending simply because it enables them to better understand firm-specific risk, or, as we stipulate, because it enables them to learn more about industry dynamics and zombie borrowers’ externalities on healthy borrowers. If the former is true, then the interaction between industry specialization and our zombie indicator in the baseline regression was spurious because of the zombie indicator being related to hard firm characteristics that reveal high risk. If the latter is true, then adding the interactions between specialization and the hard firm characteristics should not affect the interaction between zombie and specialization. The hard-information variables include the firm’s lagged total assets, leverage, return on assets, current ratio, and age. The results of these analyses are shown in columns 2, 4, and 6 ofTable 8. These richer specifications show that bank specialization remains an important and significant determinant of zombie lending, even when we explicitly account for other common sources of soft and hard information about the firm (and excluding the firm exposure from the specialization measure). The results of this exercise support the conclusion that higher industry specialization provides the bank with a separate source of proprietary information, which is bank-industry specific and soft in nature.
article.7. Benefits for Banks: Thus far, we focused exclusively on credit supply to healthy and zombie firms and the role of bank specialization. Now, we will examine the implications of zombie congestion and bank specialization in a given industry for the performance of banks’ healthy borrowers loan portfolio in that industry.15In particular, we aim to provide an answer to the following two questions. Do banks’ healthy borrowers loan portfolios suffer from zombie congestion externalities? Do banks’ healthy borrowers loan portfolios benefit from specializing in lending to an industry? To do so, we focus on a subperiod of the original sample, namely 2012–2018. As of 2012, the credit register contains information on the expectedProbability of Default(PD) of borrowers as estimated by their bank, as well as information on how much of a loan is secured by collateral (Collateral Rate). We use this information to compute the average expected default probability and average collateral rate at the bank-industry-year level for the bank’s healthy borrowers. Interest rate information is not available in the credit register, but we can proxy theCost of Debtby scaling a firm’s interest payments (from the income statement) by its bank borrowing (from the balance sheet) and take the average at bank-industry-year level for the bank’s healthy borrowers. We can then compute the expectedRisk Adjusted Returnof the loan portfolio at the bank-industry-year level as (PD × Collateral Rate) + (1 − PD) × (1 + Cost of Debt). We then analyze whether the (risk-adjusted) performance of banks’ healthy borrowers loan portfolios in different industries is related to banks’ industry specialization and the degree of zombie congestion. To get additional insights (on differences in the mechanisms), we also investigate whether banks’ industry market share matters. We run the following regression model (4): To facilitate the economic interpretation, we standardized the continuous variables in the regressions (to have a mean of zero and a standard deviation of one). We include industry fixed effects and year fixed effects in these analyses to filter out time-invariant industry characteristics and time-specific factors common to all banks. We also include four variables that control for important characteristics of the loan portfolio at the bank-industry-year level: the average equity-to-assets ratio, the average EBITDA-to-assets ratio, the average size, and the average age of the borrowers. We first focus on the impact exerted by zombie congestion on banks’ healthy borrowers loan portfolio performance. Controlling for time and industry fixed effects, we find in column 1 ofTable 9that a higher share of labor stuck in zombie firms in a given industry is associated with a lower cost of debt of healthy borrowers (and hence a lower interest income for banks). This could potentially be explained by a lower demand for credit from healthy firms, all else equal, if they have to compete with more zombie firms in an industry. In column 2, it can be seen that banks attach a higher probability of default to healthy borrowers if there is more zombie congestion in an industry. This is consistent with healthy firms’ margins suffering from zombie congestion. Indeed, zombie congestion has been shown to put pressure on healthy firms by keeping wages in the industry too high and markups too low (Acharya et al. 2024). This risk-adjusted return (column 4) combines the impact on the cost of debt, the probability of default, and the collateral rate in one metric that captures the return to the bank of granting a loan to a healthy borrower. The risk-adjusted return on lending to healthy firms is significantly negatively related to zombie congestion. Banks thus make a lower unit profit on healthy firms’ outstanding credit if the zombie congestion in an industry is higher. This indicates that banks have a clear incentive to eliminate zombie congestion as it has externalities affecting banks’ returns of their healthy borrowers’ loan portfolio. We now turn to the role of banks’ industry specialization and market share. What stands out in column 1 ofTable 9is that industry specialization is not related to higher interest rates, that is, borrowers of banks that are specialized in the industry do not have a higher cost of debt than borrowers of banks that are less specialized in the same industry. In contrast, industry market share does seem to be related to higher interest rates, even after controlling for the financial and nonfinancial characteristics of the average healthy borrower in a bank’s industry portfolio. These results suggest that market share gives banks market power enabling them to extract rents, whereas specialization does not, consistent withDe Jonghe et al. (2020). A second observation that stands out in column 2 is that banks that are more specialized in an industry attach a lower probability of default to healthy borrowers than banks that are less specialized in that industry.Blickle et al. (2021)also document that specialization leads to loans with a lower rating (i.e., safer firms). The results in column 3 ofTable 9show that banks that are more specialized in an industry also adjust lending standards by requesting less collateral from their healthy borrowers than banks that are less specialized. This is in line withGopal (2021), who finds that borrowers with a given type of collateral enjoy more generous credit supply (and hence, a higher loan amount per dollar collateral) if their bank is more specialized in handling that type of collateral. Finally, column 4 shows the consequences of the findings in columns 1 to 3 for the risk-adjusted performance of the bank’s healthy borrowers loan portfolio. Both market share and specialization help banks obtain higher risk-adjusted returns. Importantly, the mechanisms through which this happens are different.
article.8. Robustness Tests of the Baseline Spec: In this section, we discuss several robustness checks on the baseline specification (i.e.,Equation (1)andTable 3). For space and clarity, we keep this section intentionally focused and put all tables in Online Appendix J. We focus on eight potential concerns with the baseline specification: (i) endogeneous variation in bank specialization and zombie firms, (ii) firm-bank matching and credit demand, (iii) zombies versus poor quality firms, (iv) subsidized credit, (v) measurement of bank specialization, (vi) aggregate effects, (vii) the role of bank health and crisis episodes, and (viii) clustering of standard errors. 8.1. Endogenous Variation in the Main Independent Variables Whether a firm is a zombie firm or not might be a function of previous bank lending, that is, previous loan volumes and loan terms or a previous decision not to originate a loan, implying a potentially endogenous relationship. A second possible concern could be the fact that banks’ industry specialization is often an endogenous choice. In this section we attempt to exploit exogenous variation in these two main explanatory variables: banks’ industry specialization and the zombie-firm indicator. In line with the literature (Fraisse et al. 2018,Degryse et al. 2021), we try to exploit mergers and acquisitions (M&As) between banks as a way to obtain exogenous variation in industry specialization. Unfortunately, there is only one M&A that took place during our sample period, and it is between two relatively small banks. In 2013, the Belgian bank Landbouwkrediet (translation: Agricultural Credit) acquired Centea Bank and changed its name into Crelan Bank. Nonetheless, we use this merger as a source of exogenous variation in bank industry specialization and set up a test where we compare how Landbouwkrediet adjusts its lending behavior to zombie firms after its industry specialization changed because of the acquisition of Centea Bank, relative to other banks (whose industry specialization did not change) that lend to the same borrowers. The results, which are reported in Table J.1 in the Online Appendix, confirm the baseline results, although this test involves less than 2% of the observations of the full sample. Next, we exploit two separate identification approaches that yield exogenous variation in the zombie-firm indicator. First, we exploit a firm-specific shock that occurred toward the end of our sample period, namely a legal change in Belgium that significantly increased firing costs of blue-collar workers, as inCaggese et al. (2022). Second, we exploit an industry-specific shock that occurred just before the start of our sample period, namely the bursting of the dotcom bubble. In both cases, we identify affected firms that become a zombie firm after the shock, but were healthy before, and analyze whether banks’ industry specialization matters for lending to these “exogenous” zombie firms vis-à-vis firms that stayed healthy. The results of these tests are shown in Tables J.2–J.5 in the Online Appendix and are fully in line with the baseline results. 8.2. Firm-Bank Matching and Credit Demand The baselineEquation (1)includes Firm × Year fixed effects and thus controls for any observed and unobserved heterogeneity at the firm-year level. However, that does not rule out potential distortions due to (historic) firm-bank matching.16Moreover, we look at total credit at the firm-bank level, which pools together various products that differ in the degree to which they are callable by the bank (due to differences in maturity, covenants, etc.). Biases in the estimation results might thus also occur if the choice of bank products by firms is correlated with bank specialization (and would result in bank-specific credit demand, as inParavisini et al. 2023). To mitigate these concerns, we proceed in three steps. We first redo the analysis on one specific product that is homogeneous across banks and firms, namelyoverdraft facilities. At the expense of sample size and generalizability, we increase identification.17The results in columns 1, 3, and 5 of Table J.6 in the Online Appendix show that our results also apply for this homogeneous product and a much smaller sample of firms that have such a product with at least two banks. Subsequently, we add two other sets of fixed effects to this specification applied to this homogeneous product (overdraft facilities). LikePaligorova and Santos (2017),Altavilla et al. (2020), andSchivardi et al. (2022), we include Firm × Bank fixed effects to control for nonrandom matching between firms and banks. In addition, we also include Bank × Industry × Year fixed effects to further strengthen identification (e.g., capturing bank-industry–specific credit supply shocks, such as capital requirements due to concentration risk). Columns 2, 4, and 6 of Table J.6 in the Online Appendix show that the impact of specialization on credit supply to zombie firms is negative and significant (at the intensive margin) in a specification on a homogeneous product with fixed effects that control for bank-firm matching.18 Third and finally,Paravisini et al. (2023)motivate the existence of multiple banking relationships as a consequence of the multiactivity nature of the firm. Firms active in different activities will engage with different (specialized) banks depending on the financing needs for each activity. Shocks in one activity may then lead to heterogeneous credit demand, which would invalidate the assumption underlying the inclusion of Firm × Year fixed effects. To that end, we run a robustness test where we exclude multiactivity firms. In the most strict setup, we only include firms that operate in a single activity throughout the entire sample period (columns 1, 3, and 5 of Table J.8 in the Online Appendix). In columns 2, 4 and 6, we additionally include firms that have multiple activities, but all of them are in the same NACE two-digit industry. Again, we find results similar to the baseline, confirming that we identify credit supply effects. 8.3. Zombies vs. Low-Quality Firms Acharya et al. (2024)document that zombie firms constitute less than 25% of all low-quality firms. Likewise, we find that only 30% of the firms identified as zombies according to the commonly used OECD definition are still labeled zombies after our adjustments. Moreover, comparing these along many relevant dimensions (Table 2) indicates that low-quality, nonzombie firms (ZOECDZOECD= 1 and Z = 0) are more akin to healthy firms than to zombie firms. Hence, we would expect that the impact of information acquisition (through specialization) on credit supply to low-quality firms is more similar to the baseline effect (i.e., for healthy firms) than the effect for zombie borrowers. In Table J.9 in the Online Appendix, we show results of augmenting the baseline specification with an interaction term of bank specialization and a dummy that is one for low-quality, nonzombie firms and zero otherwise (i.e., a dummy capturing the firms for whichZOECDZOECD= 1 and Z = 0). We find that the coefficient in the second line has the expected negative sign but is only significant in the first column. Moreover, even in the first column, the moderating effect is substantially weaker for the lower quality firms (−0.53) than for the zombie firms (−1.58). An important insight from this additional analysis is that using measures that pool low-quality firms with zombie firms might lead to biased results and incorrect conclusions on how banks use their information advantage to reallocate credit from zombies toward healthier firms in industries in which they are specialized. 8.4. Subsidized Zombie Credit The results presented inTable 2suggest that zombie firms pay a higher interest rate than healthy higher-quality firms. This is in line with the theoretical and empirical evidence from, respectively,Faria-e-Castro et al. (2024)andFavara et al. (2021), but different fromCaballero et al. (2008)andAcharya et al. (2019), who rely on a strong concept of subsidized credit to identify zombie firms. However, as argued previously, we believe that our interest rate results are still consistent with a weaker concept of subsidized credit, whereby zombie firms pay an interest rate that is higher than healthy firms, but lower than what would be justified by their default probability. Nonetheless, we show that our main results hold even when we use alternative zombie definitions. First, we take our preferred zombie definition and split zombie firms defined by our definition into two equal groups based on the median value of the (implicit) interest rate paid by the zombies. This way we assume that it is more likely that zombie firms with the cost of debt below the median level are benefiting from subsidized credit. We then re-estimate our baseline model (1) and compare the two groups of zombie borrowers with the healthy borrowers. Estimates presented in Table J.10 in the Online Appendix suggest that the negative relationship between bank specialization and zombie lending is equally strong for all zombie companies no matter the cost of debt. Second, we followAcharya et al. (2019,2024) and compute an alternative zombie definition. According to this approach, a firm needs to satisfy two conditions to be considered a zombie. The first one is that it needs to be a low-quality firm (BB-rated or worse), which is defined as a firm whose interest coverage ratio (ICR = EBIT/Interest Expenses) is below 3.5. The second condition states that the firm needs to pay an interest rate that is below the interest rate paid by the best firms in the economy (AA-rated or better), and hence enjoys subsidized credit. These best firms are defined as firms whose ICR equals or exceeds 9.5, and the benchmark interest rate is computed at the industry-year level. We then use this alternative zombie definition to re-estimate baseline model (1) and present the results in Table J.11 in the Online Appendix. As one can see, the point estimates for the effect of bank specialization on zombie lending are also negative with this zombie definition. At the intensive margin, the relationship becomes statistically insignificant (although with apof 0.102 for the effect onΔ⁢𝐿⁢𝑛⁡(𝐶𝑟𝑒𝑑𝑖𝑡)Δ⁢L⁢n⁡(Credit)), but at the extensive margin, it remains strongly statistically significant. The results of this last robustness indicate that the relationship between bank industry specialization and zombie lending is a bit weaker when using a zombie classification based onAcharya et al. (2024). This is mainly because there is a large group of firms that we do not label as zombies but would be zombies using the Acharya classification. However, the performance characteristics of these firms (Z = 0 andZAcharyaZAcharya= 1) is more akin to the group of healthy firms (Table J.12 in the Online Appendix). 8.5. Measuring Specialization In Table J.13 in the Online Appendix, we present results using alterations of the independent variable of interest: banks’ industry specialization. In Panel A, we create a dummy𝑆𝑝𝑒𝑐𝑖𝑎𝑙𝑖𝑧𝑎𝑡𝑖𝑜𝑛(𝑡𝑜𝑝𝑑𝑒𝑐𝑖𝑙𝑒)𝑏𝑗𝑡−1Specializationbjt−1(topdecile)that takes the value of one if a bank’s industry specialization is in the top decile of industryjin yeart− 1, and zero otherwise. SeeSection 6.1for more details on the construction of this variable. Second, specialization is a proxy for the industry-specific soft information that banks acquire. However, theoretically, a large exposure to an industry could be driven by a handful of large borrowers, limiting the scope of learning through repeated interaction with firms in a given industry. Therefore, we alternatively compute𝑆𝑝𝑒𝑐𝑖𝑎𝑙𝑖𝑧𝑎𝑡𝑖𝑜𝑛(#𝑏𝑜𝑟𝑟𝑜𝑤𝑒𝑟𝑠)𝑏𝑗𝑡−1Specializationbjt−1(#borrowers)based on the number of borrowers rather than the volume lent in Panel B. More specifically, we compute this as the ratio of the number of borrowers bankbhas in a industryjrelative to the total number of borrowers bankbhas in a given year. We obtain similar results as the baseline, which is not surprising given that this measure is strongly correlated with the baseline specialization measure (corr= 0.8). Third, we use an alternative measure of information gathering, labeled expertise, based on how many years a given industry has been among the top industries of specialization of the bank.19The proxy counts (and thus adds) each consecutive year thatSpecialization(𝑡𝑜𝑝𝑑𝑒𝑐𝑖𝑙𝑒)(topdecile)is one, and is (re)set to zero whenever the dummy indicates that a bank does not (or no longer) belong to the top decile.
