The results of this test are reported in Panel C and indicate that banks with more expertise tend to provide more credit to firms operating in industries in which the bank is experienced. The results are similar when usingln(Expertise). Fourth, using two-digit NACE codes might be too granular, and acquired information might be useful for making decisions in related industries, for instance, sharing the same letter code. Measuring𝑆𝑝𝑒𝑐𝑖𝑎𝑙𝑖𝑧𝑎𝑡𝑖𝑜𝑛(𝑁𝐴𝐶𝐸𝑙𝑒𝑡𝑡𝑒𝑟𝑐𝑜𝑑𝑒)𝑏𝑗𝑡−1Specializationbjt−1(NACElettercode)in bank lending at the NACE letter code level (16 industries) in Panel C, we show again that our main findings are robust to this alteration. Fifth and finally, in line with the previous literature (De Jonghe et al. 2020,Blickle et al. 2021), we compute our measure of a bank’s industry specialization as the portfolio share of bankb’s lending to industryjincluding lending to firmi. Therefore, it might be difficult to interpret to what extent a change in bankb’s lending to zombie firms is driven by an information advantage about zombie congestion or driven by exposure to zombie firmi. Therefore, we computeSpecialization(𝑒𝑥𝑐𝑙𝑖)𝑖𝑏𝑗𝑡−1ibjt−1(excli)as the ratio of the total loan volume of bankbto industryj, excluding the loan volume to firmi, relative to the total loan volume of bankbin a given year. As can be seen in Panel D, our main findings are robust to this alteration. 8.6. Aggregate Effects The baseline results come from an unweighted regression that gives equal weight to each observation. To account for the importance of each observation and get closer to the aggregate impact, we estimate weighted regressions (ceteris paribus) using the firm’s total assets as weights. In Panel A of Table J.14 in the Online Appendix, the coefficients of the interaction effect are slightly smaller but are still significantly different from zero. The inclusion of Firm × Year fixed effects is common practice in studies that use credit register data as it allows separating demand and supply effects. However, it implies that we restrict the sample to multiple-bank firms. In the Belgian credit market, most borrowers have a single-bank relationship, which may cast doubt on the generalizability of the results. FollowingDegryse et al. (2019), we bin together single-bank firms in sets of firms that can be assumed to have similar credit demand. In particular, we create, for each year, triplets based on the firm’s region, firm’s industry, and zombie status. Using these Region × Industry × Zombie × Year fixed effects to control for single-bank borrowers’ credit demand expands the sample with a factor of 2.5. Trading of identification with representativeness, we still find quantitatively and qualitatively similar results (see Panel B of Table J.14 in the Online Appendix). 8.7. Bank Health and Crisis Periods We are the first to focus on the role of information acquisition (through industry specialization) on the credit supply to zombie firms. Previous research mainly focused on bank health and bank crises as a crucial factor in heterogeneous lending decisions toward healthy, less productive, and zombie firms. For example,Albertazzi and Marchetti (2010)andSchivardi et al. (2022)document that undercapitalized banks in Italy were more likely to keep lending to low-quality borrowers during the financial crisis in 2008. Likewise,Giannetti and Simonov (2013)show that bank undercapitalization fosters lending to zombie firms in Japan. The rationale here is that banks that struggle to meet regulatory capital requirements will be less willing to recognize losses on bad loans. Next to bank capital, also profitability (Bonfim et al. 2023) and bank size (Albertazzi and Marchetti 2010) have been shown to affect zombie lending. As bank specialization might be correlated with bank capital, profitability, and size(Beck et al. 2022), it is important to test whether bank specialization remains an important channel affecting zombie lending despite including an interaction with the other important drivers of zombie lending. Similarly, the recovery from a (financial) crisis usually features an increasing share of zombies, a strategic reorientation by banks, and a credit contraction. It is thus important to verify whether our results are time varying and episode specific. We use a fairly long time period to make the results as general as possible but are able to analyze whether there is a differential impact in (i) the period prior to the global financial crisis (2004–2007), (ii) the period spanning the global financial crisis and the sovereign debt crisis (2008–2013), and (iii) the postcrisis period (2014–2018). Including interactions of the zombie dummy with various bank characteristics and triple interactions between bank specialization, zombie status, and (post)crisis period dummies leave the coefficients of interest largely unaffected. If anything, in Table J.15 in the Online Appendix, we even notice an economically larger effect. 8.8. Clustering of Standard Errors The baseline results come from clustering standard errors at the bank-industry level and thus at the cross of banks and industries. A potential issue is that observations within the same industry across banks are likely related to each other. To address this issue, we re-estimate the baselineEquation (1)and double-cluster standard errors at the industry and bank levels. Table J.16 in the Online Appendix presents the results of the estimation using this double clustering of standard errors. The standard errors (and hence significance of the point estimates) differ little from those presented inTable 3.
article.9. Conclusion: This paper contributes to the discussion of zombie congestion and zombie lending using loan-level data for the universe of firms and banks in Belgium between 2004 and 2018. We develop a new approach to identify zombie firms and uncover a previously unexplored determinant of zombie lending, namely bank specialization. Overall, our findings convey two interesting and important messages. First, the choice of how zombie firms are identified is important as it may lead to substantial differences in the estimated zombie population, both in terms of the number and quality of zombie firms. Compared with the widely used OECD definition (McGowan et al. 2018), our approach identifies much fewer firms as zombies (3.4% of the population instead of 10.4%), and they are of much lower quality than healthy firms. Nonetheless, despite documenting that there are relatively fewer zombie firms in Belgium than what other studies have found, we find that (i) some industries have significant above average amounts of zombie firms and (ii) having more resources stuck in zombie firms correlates negatively with the performance of banks’ healthy borrowers portfolio. Second, we provide evidence on the effect that bank industry specialization has on zombie lending. We show that bank specialization is associated with lower and even reduced credit supply to zombie borrowers. Banks that are specialized in lending to a certain industry have gained an information advantage that makes them more aware of zombie firms in the industry as well as more knowledgeable about the negative impact that zombie firms exert on healthy borrowers. Our analysis shows that this information channel is indeed industry-specific and different from borrower-specific hard or soft information. Moreover, specialized banks reduce zombie lending even more when the scope and opportunity cost of negative externalities on healthy borrowers is larger; namely, when the fraction of industry labor stuck in zombie firms is larger, when the industry is geographically more concentrated, or when the industry is expected to have better investment opportunities. Additionally, specialized banks reduce zombie lending more in industries with low than with high asset specificity. In industries with high asset specificity, it is relatively more likely that a zombie firms’ default would lead to its assets being sold at a significant discount and hence more likely to contaminate the value of healthy borrowers’ collateral. Our results imply that bank specialization can benefit financial stability as it reduces zombie lending. As such, it not only improves the quality of the bank’s lending portfolio by increasing the relative share of credit going to healthy borrowers, it also improves the business climate for the healthy borrowers who then compete with fewer or smaller zombie firms. Our study, however, does not claim that a lending specialization of 100% should be preferred for banks as we only provide a partial analysis rather than a general equilibrium approach. Our results merely show that there are also benefits to divert from a full diversification. Our findings are particularly relevant for turbulent times. The COVID-19 pandemic and the steep rise in energy and commodity prices in 2021–2022 had put a severe strain on the liquidity of various corporations. In such circumstances, an increase in nonperforming loans (NPLs) can be expected, making it important to differentiate between illiquid, yet viable firms and zombie firms. Therefore, banks’ expertise and screening abilities become especially important to identify truly viable firms and negotiate the term of debt restructuring in a way that supports the economic recovery. In parallel, improvements in insolvency regime to ensure more efficient corporate restructuring can further mitigate the negative impact of credit misallocation (Becker and Ivashina 2022). Finally, although “killing” zombie firms would improve resource allocation and bring about productivity gains, this should likely not happen overnight (Schivardi et al. 2022). Despite the economic gains that can be expected to follow in the long run when zombie firms exit the market, a sudden closure of multiple enterprises may have a negative effect on the economy. It may cause capital destruction and negative spillovers to firms both within and outside the industry where the closures are happening. Indeed, sudden mass closure of businesses may lead to fire sales (Shleifer and Vishny 1992) of assets and collateral below fair prices, driving down the collateral value of other firms in the industry and exacerbating their financial constraints (Kiyotaki and Moore 1997). Additionally, fire sales harm firms’ creditors (Acharya et al. 2007) and may have a negative spillover to other industries via the supply chain channel (Giannetti and Saidi 2019). Future research could focus on the effects that zombie firms have along the supply chain. Furthermore, bank specialization may also play a role in the context of input-output linkages. Banks specialized in an industry may, for instance, lend more to zombies in downstream industries to preserve demand for the output of the borrowers in their specialized industry, or similar to zombies in upstream industries not to jeopardize supply chains. The superior information gained in an industry may also make banks better able to identify the zombies in other industries that are crucial to support the healthy firms operating in the industry in which the bank is mainly specialized. To conclude, we voice the suggestion ofSchivardi et al. (2020)that future research focused on the real effects of zombie congestion should emphasize exogenous variation in the number of zombie firms to determine whether and how zombie lending may slow a recovery.
